name: Simple Interest

sourceRefs.label: 2024_q page 33 Q48 (doubling-time problem, with answer key)
2022_q1 page 31 Q144 (basic SI calculation, with answer key)
2024_q page 63 Q97 (SI with a mixed-fraction rate and month-based time, with answer key)
2025_q1 page 125 Q191 (two-loan comparison word problem, with answer key — already used in the Simplification topic, re-noted here as it's equally a Simple Interest problem)

researchNotes: 4 genuine real past-paper hits across 3 years (2022, 2024, 2025), covering 4 distinct shapes: doubling-time, plain calculation, fractional-rate-with-months, and two-loan comparison. Strong evidence base — only 2 authored items added to round out coverage, both clearly tagged. Expanded 2026-07 pass (append-only — also used Day 6, already completed): added the generalized 'sum becomes k times' formula (covering tripling/quadrupling, not just doubling), day-based time conversion, the equal-SI inverse-ratio shortcut for split sums, and several classic two-rate/two-time-period problem shapes. Every mock question, new and pre-existing, now carries the mandatory Part-B `trick`. All arithmetic re-verified by hand before finalizing. Round-2 pass (2026-07, second expansion): added the 'rate and time numerically equal' problem shape and the sequential-reinvestment-at-a-different-rate shape (distinct from the existing equal-split and two-party-comparison shapes). Arithmetic re-verified; one placeholder token accidentally left in an early draft of m18 was caught and corrected before finalizing.

reading.intro: Simple Interest is 1 of 10 named Aptitude sub-skills. Real exam evidence shows TNPSC favors word-problem variants (doubling time, comparing two loans, fractional rates with month-based time) over the bare 'calculate SI' formula alone.

qa.question: Formula: What is the Simple Interest formula, and how do you get the doubling time from it?
qa.answer: SI = (Principal × Rate × Time) / 100. A sum DOUBLES when SI equals the Principal itself: P = (P×R×T)/100 → T = 100/R years. So at 8% per annum, doubling time = 100/8 = 12.5 years = 150 months.
qa.tip: T = 100/R is the doubling-time formula — memorize it directly rather than re-deriving it each time.

qa.question: Real exam (2024): In how many months will Rs.2,000 double itself at 8% per annum simple interest?
Real exam (2022): What is the simple interest on Rs.1,000 for one year at 10% per annum?
Real exam (2024): Find the simple interest on Rs.68,000 at 16⅔% per annum for 9 months.
qa.answer: Convert the mixed-fraction rate: 16⅔% = 50/3 %. Convert months to years: 9 months = 9/12 = 3/4 year. SI = 68,000 × (50/3) × (3/4) / 100 = 68,000 × 12.5/100 = Rs.8,500. (The 3s cancel neatly between the rate's denominator and the time's numerator — recognizing that shortcut avoids messy fraction arithmetic.)

qa.question: Real exam (2025): A borrows Rs.16,000 at 12% p.a. simple interest, B borrows Rs.12,000 at 18% p.a. simple interest. In how many years will their total amounts owed (principal+interest) become equal?
Method — finding the rate when given principal, time, and final amount: A sum of Rs.5,000 becomes Rs.6,200 in 3 years at simple interest. Find the rate.
qa.answer: SI = Amount − Principal = 6200−5000 = 1200. Rearranged formula: R = (SI×100)/(P×T) = (1200×100)/(5000×3) = 120000/15000 = 8% per annum.

qa.question: What is the general formula for the time taken for a sum to become 'k times' itself at simple interest (not just double)?
qa.answer: If a sum becomes k times itself, SI = (k−1)×Principal (since Amount = k×P, so SI = kP−P = (k−1)P). Time = 100×(k−1)/R years. Doubling (k=2) gives the familiar T=100/R; tripling (k=3) gives T=200/R; quadrupling (k=4) gives T=300/R.
qa.tip: One master formula covers doubling, tripling, and beyond: T = 100(k−1)/R — don't memorize a separate formula for each multiple.

qa.question: A sum triples itself in 20 years at simple interest. Find the rate.
How do you handle simple interest problems where the time period is given in days rather than months or years?
qa.answer: Convert days to a year-fraction by dividing by 365 (the standard convention unless a problem explicitly states otherwise). E.g., 73 days = 73/365 = 1/5 of a year.

qa.question: Method — splitting a sum so two parts earn equal SI at different rates/times: A sum is divided into two parts such that the SI on the first part at 8% for 5 years equals the SI on the second part at 10% for 4 years. Find the ratio of the two parts.
qa.answer: Set Part1×8×5 = Part2×10×4 → 40×Part1 = 40×Part2 → Part1:Part2 = 1:1. When SI is equal, the two parts are in the INVERSE ratio of their own (Rate×Time) products.
qa.tip: Equal-SI split-sum shortcut: Part1:Part2 = (Rate2×Time2) : (Rate1×Time1) — the inverse of each part's own rate-time product.

qa.question: Method — finding Principal from two different future amounts: A sum amounts to Rs.9,200 in 4 years and Rs.10,400 in 6 years at simple interest. Find the Principal.
qa.answer: The extra 2 years add Rs.10,400−9,200 = Rs.1,200 of interest, so annual SI = Rs.600. Principal = Amount after 4 years − (4 × annual SI) = 9,200 − 2,400 = Rs.6,800.
qa.tip: The difference between two given amounts, divided by the gap in years, gives the annual SI directly — then subtract (years × annual SI) from either amount to isolate the Principal.

qa.question: What fraction of the Principal does the SI represent after T years at R% per annum, and why is this useful?
qa.answer: SI as a fraction of Principal = (R×T)/100 — the Principal itself cancels out of the ratio, so you can answer 'what fraction/percentage of the sum is the interest' without ever knowing the actual Principal value.

qa.question: Round-2 pass — Method: the rate and time are numerically equal. A sum is lent at simple interest where the rate percent per annum and the time in years are numerically equal (both = x). If the SI is 1/9 of the sum, find x.
qa.answer: SI = P×R×T/100 = P×x×x/100 (since R=T=x). Set SI = P/9: x²/100 = 1/9 → x² = 100/9 → x = 10/3 = 3⅓. So the rate (and time) is 3⅓%(years).
qa.tip: Whenever a problem says 'rate and time are numerically equal', substitute R=T=x into SI=PRT/100 to get a single-variable quadratic in x — a distinct shortcut from the usual three-separate-values setup.

qa.question: Method — same sum invested at two DIFFERENT rates back-to-back (sequential periods, not a split sum): Rs.4,000 is invested for 2 years at 6% per annum, then the resulting amount is reinvested for 3 more years at 8% per annum simple interest. Find the final amount.
qa.answer: First period: SI = 4000×6×2/100 = 480. Amount after period 1 = 4,480. This new amount becomes the Principal for period 2: SI = 4480×8×3/100 = 1,075.20. Final amount = 4480+1075.20 = Rs.5,555.20.
qa.tip: For sequential (back-to-back) simple-interest periods at different rates, the amount at the end of one period becomes the NEW principal for the next period — don't just add both periods' interest onto the original principal.

questions.prompt: In how many years will a sum double itself at 5% per annum simple interest?
questions.trick: T = 100/R for doubling — an instant formula, no need to set up SI=P from scratch.

questions.prompt: Find the simple interest on Rs.2,500 for 1 year at 12% per annum.
questions.trick: Plug straight into SI=PRT/100 for single-year, whole-number-rate problems — no shortcuts needed for the simplest case.

questions.prompt: Find the simple interest on Rs.54,000 at 13⅓% per annum for 6 months.
questions.trick: Convert the mixed-fraction rate to an improper fraction AND convert months to a year-fraction before multiplying anything — doing both conversions upfront avoids messy intermediate fractions.

questions.prompt: C borrows Rs.20,000 at 10% p.a. simple interest, D borrows Rs.15,000 at 15% p.a. simple interest. In how many years will their total amounts owed become equal?
questions.trick: Set Principal+Principal×Rate×t/100 equal for both parties and solve directly for t — never guess-and-check candidate years.

questions.prompt: A sum of Rs.8,000 becomes Rs.9,440 in 3 years at simple interest. Find the rate per annum.
questions.trick: R = SI×100/(P×T), where SI = Amount − Principal — a direct rearrangement of the base formula.

questions.prompt: What sum will yield Rs.900 as simple interest in 3 years at 6% per annum?
questions.trick: P = SI×100/(R×T) — the same base formula rearranged to solve for Principal.

questions.prompt: A sum triples itself in 25 years at simple interest. Find the rate.
questions.trick: Use T=100(k−1)/R for any 'becomes k times' problem — here k=3 gives 200/R.

questions.prompt: In how many years will a sum become 4 times itself at 15% per annum simple interest?
questions.trick: T=100(k−1)/R: for quadrupling, k−1=3, so T=300/R.

questions.prompt: A sum is divided into two parts such that the SI on the first part at 8% for 5 years equals the SI on the second part at 10% for 4 years. Find the ratio of the two parts.
questions.trick: Equal SI → parts are in INVERSE ratio of their own Rate×Time products.

questions.prompt: Find the simple interest on Rs.72,000 for 219 days at 10% per annum (use a 365-day year).
questions.trick: Convert days to a year-fraction (÷365) before applying SI=PRT/100.

questions.prompt: What fraction of the Principal is the simple interest earned over 4 years at 5% per annum?
questions.trick: SI/Principal = RT/100 — Principal cancels, so you never need its actual value.

questions.prompt: A sum amounts to Rs.9,200 in 4 years and Rs.10,400 in 6 years at simple interest. Find the Principal.
questions.trick: Difference in amounts ÷ difference in years = annual SI; then subtract (years×annual SI) from either given amount.

questions.prompt: Find the rate of interest if a sum becomes double in 8 years at simple interest.
questions.trick: R=100/T — the doubling formula rearranged for rate instead of time.

questions.prompt: Rs.15,000 is lent partly at 10% and partly at 8% simple interest, yielding total annual interest of Rs.1,280. Find the amount lent at 10%.
questions.trick: Let one part be x, the other (total−x) — one linear equation is enough, no need for two variables.

questions.prompt: The SI on a sum for 2 years at 5% is Rs.500 less than the SI on the same sum for 5 years at 4%. Find the sum.
questions.trick: Express each SI as a decimal fraction of P (0.1P, 0.2P style) and subtract — the P's combine into one simple equation.

questions.prompt: If the rate of interest is increased by 3%, a sum yields Rs.1,350 more as SI over 5 years. Find the sum.
questions.trick: A rate increase of r% over T years adds exactly P×r×T/100 in extra SI — a direct shortcut skipping two full SI calculations.

questions.prompt: A sum at simple interest amounts to Rs.1,120 in 4 years and Rs.1,200 in 5 years. Find the rate percent.
questions.trick: Difference between consecutive-year amounts = annual SI directly; then back-solve Principal and Rate from there.

questions.prompt: A sum is lent at simple interest where the rate percent per annum and the time in years are numerically equal. If the SI is 4/25 of the sum, find the rate.
questions.trick: Substitute R=T=x into SI=PRT/100 to reduce to a single-variable equation in x.

questions.prompt: Rs.6,000 is invested for 2 years at 5% per annum simple interest, then the resulting amount is reinvested for 2 more years at 10% per annum simple interest. Find the final amount.
questions.trick: The amount at the end of one SI period becomes the new principal for the next sequential period — never add both periods' interest onto the ORIGINAL principal.
